EUR.EUR/USD: The Euro remains structurally capped near 1.1432 as a string of disappointing domestic data releases drags the Eurozone Economic Surprise Index down to a net negative 16. The pair is also reacting to the intraday catalyst of Commerzbank reporting oil supply rebounds and glut concerns, which has eased broader greenback momentum. The ECB remains on hold ahead of its July 23 meeting, while the Fed is also on hold for its July 29 decision
EUR.EUR/GBP: EUR/GBP is trading softer as the single currency is weighed down by softer regional inflation data and a declining Eurozone Economic Surprise Index. Conversely, the British Pound is supported by hawkish rhetoric from Bank of England policymaker Catherine Mann, who highlighted persistent services inflation and the loosening of financial conditions. Both the ECB and the BoE are on hold ahead of their respective July policy meetings
EUR.EUR/JPY: EUR/JPY is supported by the persistent weakness of the Japanese Yen, which has touched fresh multi-decade lows due to a massive negative carry profile. The ECB remains on hold for its July 23 meeting, while the BoJ is also on hold for its July 31 meeting. JPY COT LF short -115.0k. BoJ on hold

The Euro is under pressure from a declining Eurozone Economic Surprise Index and softer regional inflation data. Both the ECB and the RBA are on hold ahead of their upcoming summer policy decisions

The ECB remains on hold for its July 23 meeting, while the BoC is also on hold for its July 15 meeting. CAD COT LF short -87.1k. BoC on hold
EUR.EUR/CHF: EUR/CHF remains in a tight range as the Swiss Franc experiences a corrective bounce amid a broader softening of the US Dollar. The ECB is on hold for its July 23 meeting, while the Swiss National Bank is on hold for its September 24 meeting. The pair is also shaped by slowing Swiss consumer price inflation, which cooled to 0.6% due to fading imported price pressures
EUR.EUR/NZD: EUR/NZD is trading lower as the New Zealand Dollar capitalizes on a rebound in local consumer confidence and a downward reset in inflation expectations. A rate hike is priced in for the Reserve Bank of New Zealand at its July 8 meeting, with a 62% probability of a hike. In contrast, the ECB is on hold for its July 23 meeting, leaving the Euro vulnerable to the widening policy divergence
